_readme: Use entries[] as full word records. Merge each object into Database.json by id (replace matching element).

entries.etymology: Old French abaissier, from Latin ad- ('to') + bassus ('low')
entries.exampleSentence: After being overthrown and abased, the deposed leader offered to bow down to his conqueror.

memoryHook.text: Being brought to your base (low point) can be humiliating.

entries.etymology: Old French abatre, from Latin ad- + battuere ('to beat down')
entries.exampleSentence: The rain poured down for a while, then abated.

memoryHook.text: If you don't use bait you reduce your odds in fishing.

entries.definition: To give up a position, usually one of leadership
entries.etymology: Latin abdicare, from ab- ('away') + dicare ('to proclaim')
entries.exampleSentence: When he realized that the revolutionaries would surely win, the king abdicated his throne.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "dic-" (speak) + "-ate" (verb) = to formally speak away your power.

entries.etymology: Latin abducere, from ab- ('away') + ducere ('to lead')
entries.exampleSentence: The evildoers abducted the fairy princess from her happy home.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "duct" (lead) = to lead away.

entries.definition: Something that differs from the norm
entries.etymology: Latin aberratio, from aberrare ('to wander away'), from ab- + errare ('to stray')
entries.exampleSentence: In 1918, the Boston Red Sox won the World Series, but the success turned out to be an aberration.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "err" (wander) = wandering away from what is normal.

entries.etymology: Old French abeter, from a- + beter ('to bait'), of Germanic origin
entries.exampleSentence: The spy succeeded only because he had a friend on the inside to abet him.

memoryHook.text: A bet placed on someone encourages them to succeed.

entries.etymology: Latin abhorrere, from ab- ('away from') + horrere ('to shudder')
entries.exampleSentence: Because he always wound up kicking himself in the head when he tried to play soccer, Oswald began to abhor the sport.

memoryHook.text: A horror - you hate it and shrink away from it.

entries.etymology: Old English abidan, from a- (intensive prefix) + bidan ('to wait, remain')

senses.exampleSentence: Though he did not agree with the decision, Chuck decided to abide by it.
Despite the beating they've taken from the weather throughout the millennia, the mountains abide.

memoryHook.text: You bide your time, enduring or putting up with the situation.

entries.exampleSentence: Though he did not agree with the decision, Chuck decided to abide by it.

entries.etymology: Latin abjectus, past participle of abicere ('to throw away'), from ab- + jacere ('to throw')
entries.exampleSentence: After losing all her money, falling into a puddle, and breaking her ankle, Eloise was abject.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "ject" (throw) = thrown away into a wretched state.

entries.etymology: Latin abjurare, from ab- ('away') + jurare ('to swear')
entries.exampleSentence: To prove his honesty, the President abjured the evil policies of his wicked predecessor.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "jur" (swear) = to swear away or legally renounce.

entries.etymology: Latin abnegatio, from abnegare ('to refuse'), from ab- + negare ('to deny')
entries.exampleSentence: The holy man slept on the floor, took only cold showers, and generally followed other practices of abnegation.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "neg" (deny) = the act of denying yourself something.

entries.definition: To give up on a half-finished project or effort
entries.etymology: Latin abortare, from aboriri ('to miscarry'), from ab- + oriri ('to arise, be born')
entries.exampleSentence: After they ran out of food, the men attempting to jump rope around the world had to abort and go home.

entries.etymology: Old French abregier, from Latin abbreviare ('to shorten'), from ad- + brevis ('short')

senses.exampleSentence: The publisher thought the dictionary was too long and abridged it.
Moby-Dick is such a long book that even the abridged version is longer than most normal books.

memoryHook.text: A bridge connects two points, making the journey shorter.

entries.exampleSentence: The publisher thought the dictionary was too long and abridged it.

entries.etymology: Latin abrogare, from ab- + rogare ('to propose a law, ask')
entries.exampleSentence: The Bill of Rights assures that the government cannot abrogate our right to a free press.

memoryHook.text: A broken gate means the security rules have been abolished or repealed.

entries.etymology: Latin abscondere, from abs- ('away') + condere ('to hide, store')
entries.exampleSentence: In the confusion, the super-spy absconded into the night with the secret plans.

memoryHook.text: Abscond with the scones - running away secretly with the stolen goods.

entries.etymology: Latin absolutio, from absolvere ('to set free'), from ab- + solvere ('to loosen')
entries.exampleSentence: Once all the facts were known, the jury gave Angela absolution by giving a verdict of not guilty.

memoryHook.text: The absolute solution to guilt is being forgiven completely.

entries.definition: To freely choose not to commit an action
entries.etymology: Latin abstinere, from abs- ('from') + tenere ('to hold')
entries.exampleSentence: Everyone demanded that Angus put on the kilt, but he did not want to do it and abstained.

memoryHook.text: You abstain from wine so it doesn't stain your shirt.

entries.etymology: Latin abstrusus, past participle of abstrudere ('to conceal'), from abs- + trudere ('to push')
entries.exampleSentence: Everyone else in the class understood geometry easily, but John found the subject abstruse.

memoryHook.text: Sounds like obstructs - the complex meaning obstructs your understanding.

entries.etymology: Latin accedere, from ad- ('to') + cedere ('to go, yield')
entries.exampleSentence: When the class asked the teacher whether they could play baseball instead of learn grammar, he acceded to their request.

memoryHook.text: "ac-" (toward) + "cede" (yield) = to yield toward an agreement.

entries.etymology: Medieval Latin accentuare, from Latin accentus ('accent'), from ad- + cantus ('song')
entries.exampleSentence: Psychologists agree that those people who are happiest accentuate the positive in life.

entries.etymology: Latin accessibilis, from accedere ('to approach'), from ad- + cedere ('to go')
entries.exampleSentence: After studying with SparkNotes and getting a great score on the SAT, Marlena realized her goal of getting into an Ivy-League college was accessible.

entries.etymology: Latin acclamare, from ad- + clamare ('to shout')
entries.exampleSentence: Greg's excellent poem won the acclaim of his friends.

memoryHook.text: "ac-" (toward) + "claim" (shout) = shouting praise toward someone.

entries.etymology: French accolade, from accoler ('to embrace'), from Latin ad- + collum ('neck')
entries.exampleSentence: Everyone offered accolades to Sam after he won the Nobel Prize.

memoryHook.text: From Latin "ad" (to) + "collum" (neck). Originally a ceremonial embrace around the neck when conferring knighthood.

entries.etymology: Latin accommodare, from ad- + commodus ('fitting'), from com- + modus ('measure')
entries.exampleSentence: Though the apartment was not big enough for three people, Arnold, Mark, and Zebulon were all friends and were accommodating to each other.

entries.etymology: Old French acorder, from Latin accordare, from ad- + cor ('heart')
entries.exampleSentence: After much negotiating, England and Iceland finally came to a mutually beneficial accord about fishing rights.

memoryHook.text: "ac-" (toward) + "cord" (heart) = hearts moving toward agreement.

entries.etymology: French accoster, from Italian accostare, from Latin ad- + costa ('rib, side')
entries.exampleSentence: Though Antoinette was normally quite calm, when the waiter spilled soup on her for the fourth time she stood up and accosted the man.

memoryHook.text: From Latin "ad costam" (to the rib/side). To aggressively come right up to someone's side and confront them.

entries.definition: Slow growth in size or amount
entries.etymology: Latin accretio, from accrescere ('to grow'), from ad- + crescere ('to grow')
entries.exampleSentence: Stalactites are formed by the accretion of minerals from the roofs of caves.

memoryHook.text: A creation built up gradually layer by layer.

entries.definition: Biting, bitter in tone or taste
entries.etymology: Latin acerbus ('bitter, harsh'), related to acer ('sharp')
entries.exampleSentence: Jill became extremely acerbic and began to cruelly make fun of all her friends.

memoryHook.text: An acerbic comment is acidic and bites.

entries.etymology: Latin acquiescere, from ad- + quiescere ('to be quiet'), from quies ('quiet')
entries.exampleSentence: Though Mr. Correlli wanted to stay outside and work in his garage, when his wife told him to come in to dinner, he acquiesced.

memoryHook.text: Acquire quiet - you stay quiet and just agree to it.

entries.etymology: Latin acrimonia, from acer ('sharp, bitter') + -monia (noun suffix)
entries.exampleSentence: Though they vowed that no girl would ever come between them, Biff and Trevor could not keep acrimony from overwhelming their friendship.

memoryHook.text: A crime on me - leaves you with bitterness and ill will.

entries.etymology: Latin acumen ('sharpness'), from acuere ('to sharpen'), from acus ('needle')
entries.exampleSentence: Because of his mathematical acumen, Larry was able to figure out in minutes problems that took other students hours.

memoryHook.text: IQ men - men with high IQs have great mental sharpness.

entries.etymology: Latin acutus, past participle of acuere ('to sharpen'), from acus ('needle')

senses.exampleSentence: Arnold could not walk because the pain in his foot was so acute.
Because she was so acute, Libby instantly figured out how the magician pulled off his trick.

memoryHook.text: A cute angle is sharp and precise.

entries.exampleSentence: Arnold could not walk because the pain in his foot was so acute.

entries.etymology: Latin adamans, from Greek adamas ('unconquerable'), from a- ('not') + daman ('to tame')
entries.exampleSentence: Though public pressure was intense, the President remained adamant about his proposal.

memoryHook.text: From Greek adamas, meaning "uncuttable" (the same root as diamond). Unyielding and unbreakable.

entries.etymology: Latin adeptus, past participle of adipisci ('to attain'), from ad- + apisci ('to reach')
entries.exampleSentence: Tarzan was adept at jumping from tree to tree like a monkey.

memoryHook.text: A depth of knowledge makes you highly skilled.

entries.etymology: Latin adhaerere, from ad- + haerere ('to stick')

senses.exampleSentence: We adhered the poster to the wall with tape.
He adhered to the dictates of his religion without question.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (to) + "haerere" (stick) = to stick to.

entries.exampleSentence: We adhered the poster to the wall with tape.

entries.etymology: Old French admonester, from Latin admonere ('to advise'), from ad- + monere ('to warn')
entries.exampleSentence: Joe's mother admonished him not to ruin his appetite by eating cookies before dinner.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (to) + "monere" (warn) = to give a warning to.

entries.etymology: Latin adornare, from ad- + ornare ('to furnish, decorate')
entries.exampleSentence: We adorned the tree with ornaments.

entries.etymology: French adroit, from à droit ('according to right'), from Latin directus ('straight')
entries.exampleSentence: The adroit thief could pick someone's pocket without attracting notice.

memoryHook.text: From French à droit meaning "to the right." The right hand was historically considered the skilled and dexterous one.

entries.etymology: Latin adulatio, from adulari ('to fawn on'), possibly from cauda ('tail') — as a dog wagging its tail
entries.exampleSentence: Though the book was pretty good, Marcy did not believe it deserved the adulation it received.

memoryHook.text: Adult infatuation - excessive flattery and worship.

entries.definition: To sketch out in a vague way
entries.etymology: Latin adumbrare ('to sketch'), from ad- + umbra ('shadow')
entries.exampleSentence: The coach adumbrated a game plan, but none of the players knew precisely what to do.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (toward) + "umbr" (shadow) = to cast a shadow over, or outline vaguely.

entries.etymology: Latin adversus ('turned against'), from advertere, from ad- + vertere ('to turn')
entries.exampleSentence: Because of adverse conditions, the hikers decided to give up trying to climb the mountain.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (against) + "vers" (turn) = turned against you; hostile.

entries.etymology: Latin advocare ('to call to one's aid'), from ad- + vocare ('to call')

senses.definition: To argue in favor of something
senses.exampleSentence: Arnold advocated turning left at the stop sign, even though everyone else thought we should turn right.

senses.definition: A person who argues in favor of something
senses.exampleSentence: In addition to wanting to turn left at every stop sign, Arnold was also a great advocate of increasing national defense spending.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (toward) + "voc" (voice) = to add your voice toward a cause.

entries.definition: To argue in favor of something
entries.exampleSentence: Arnold advocated turning left at the stop sign, even though everyone else thought we should turn right.

entries.etymology: Latin aerius, from Greek aerios, from aer ('air')
entries.exampleSentence: We watched as the fighter planes conducted aerial maneuvers.

entries.definition: Artistic, related to the appreciation of beauty
entries.etymology: Greek aisthetikos ('of sense perception'), from aisthanesthai ('to perceive')
entries.exampleSentence: We hired Susan as our interior decorator because she has such a fine aesthetic sense.

entries.etymology: Latin affabilis, from affari ('to speak to'), from ad- + fari ('to speak')
entries.exampleSentence: People like to be around George because he is so affable and good-natured.

memoryHook.text: A fable - the grandpa telling a fable is friendly and easy to talk to.

entries.etymology: Latin affinitas ('relationship by marriage'), from affinis ('bordering on'), from ad- + finis ('border')
entries.exampleSentence: Jerry didn't know why, but he felt an incredible affinity for Kramer the first time they met.

memoryHook.text: Infinity - an infinite, natural connection or liking for something.

entries.etymology: Latin affluens, from affluere ('to flow toward'), from ad- + fluere ('to flow')
entries.exampleSentence: Mrs. Grebelski was affluent, owning a huge house, three cars, and an island near Maine.

memoryHook.text: "af-" (toward) + "flu" (flow) = wealth flowing toward you.

entries.etymology: Old French afronter ('to slap in the face'), from Latin ad- + frons ('forehead, face')
entries.exampleSentence: Bernardo was very touchy, and took any slight as an affront to his honor.

memoryHook.text: In front - an insult right in front of your face.

entries.etymology: French agrandir, from a- + grandir ('to enlarge'), from Latin grandire, from grandis ('great')
entries.exampleSentence: Joseph always dropped the names of the famous people his father knew as a way to aggrandize his personal stature.

memoryHook.text: To make something grander.

entries.etymology: Latin aggregatus, from aggregare ('to add to a flock'), from ad- + grex ('flock')

senses.exampleSentence: The three branches of the U.S. Government form an aggregate much more powerful than its individual parts.
The dictator tried to aggregate as many people into his army as he possibly could.

memoryHook.text: "ag-" (toward) + "greg" (flock) = to bring the flock together into a whole.

entries.exampleSentence: The three branches of the U.S. Government form an aggregate much more powerful than its individual parts.

entries.etymology: Old French agrever ('to make heavier'), from Latin aggravare, from ad- + gravis ('heavy')
entries.exampleSentence: The foreman mercilessly overworked his aggrieved employees.

memoryHook.text: You feel aggrieved when someone causes you grief.

entries.etymology: Latin agilis, from agere ('to do, drive')
entries.exampleSentence: The dogs were too slow to catch the agile rabbit.

entries.definition: Believing that the existence of God cannot be proven or.
entries.etymology: Greek agnostos ('unknown'), from a- ('not') + gnostos ('known'), coined by T.H. Huxley in 1869
entries.exampleSentence: Joey's parents are very religious, but he is agnostic.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (without) + "gnos" (knowledge) = claiming to be without knowledge (usually of God).

entries.etymology: Latin agricultura, from ager ('field') + cultura ('cultivation'), from colere ('to cultivate')
entries.exampleSentence: It was a huge step in civilization when tribes left hunting and gathering and began to develop more sustainable methods of obtaining food, such as agriculture.

entries.definition: A passageway between rows of seats
entries.etymology: Old French ele ('wing of a building'), from Latin ala ('wing'); spelling influenced by isle
entries.exampleSentence: Once we got inside the stadium we walked down the aisle to our seats.

entries.etymology: Latin alacritas, from alacer ('brisk, lively')
entries.exampleSentence: For some reason, Chuck loved to help his mother whenever he could, so when his mother asked him to set the table he did so with alacrity.

memoryHook.text: Sounds like electricity - moving with electric speed and readiness.

entries.etymology: Latin alias ('otherwise, at another time'), from alius ('other')
entries.exampleSentence: He snuck past the guards by using an alias and fake ID.

entries.etymology: Old English alecgan, from a- (intensive) + lecgan ('to lay')
entries.exampleSentence: The chairman of the Federal Reserve gave a speech to try to allay investors' fears about an economic downturn.

memoryHook.text: To lay a baby down soothes and calms it.

entries.etymology: Old French alegier, from Latin allegare ('to send on a mission'), from ad- + legare ('to depute')
entries.exampleSentence: The policeman had alleged that Marshall committed the crime, but after the investigation turned up no evidence, Marshall was set free.

entries.etymology: Latin alleviare, from ad- + levis ('light'), meaning to lighten a burden
entries.exampleSentence: This drug will alleviate the symptoms of the terrible disease, but only for a while.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (to) + "lev" (light) = to make something lighter or less severe.

entries.etymology: Medieval Latin allocare, from Latin ad- + locare ('to place'), from locus ('place')
entries.exampleSentence: The Mayor allocated 30 percent of the funds for improving the town's schools.

entries.etymology: Dutch te loef ('to windward'), nautical term meaning to keep a distance; loof relates to 'luff'
entries.exampleSentence: The scientist could sometimes seem aloof, as if he didn't care about his friends or family, but really he was just thinking about quantum mechanics.

memoryHook.text: A roof - sitting alone on a roof, emotionally detached from everyone below.

entries.etymology: Latin altercatio, from altercari ('to dispute'), from alter ('other')
entries.exampleSentence: Jason and Lionel blamed one another for the car accident, leading to an altercation.

memoryHook.text: When things alter (change) for the worse, a noisy argument starts.

entries.etymology: Medieval Latin amalgamare, from amalgama ('alloy of mercury'), possibly from Greek malagma ('softening')
entries.exampleSentence: Because of his great charisma, the presidential candidate was able to amalgamate all democrats and republicans under his banner.

memoryHook.text: A mall - a mall brings many different stores together into one place.

entries.etymology: Latin ambiguus, from ambigere ('to wander around'), from ambi- ('both ways') + agere ('to drive')
entries.exampleSentence: Some people think Caesar married Cleopatra for her power, others believe he was charmed by her beauty. His actual reasons are ambiguous.

memoryHook.text: "ambi-" (both) + "agere" (drive) = driven in two directions, unclear.

entries.etymology: Latin ambi- ('both') + valentia ('strength'), coined in early 20th-century psychology
entries.exampleSentence: My feelings about Calvin are ambivalent because on one hand he is a loyal friend, but on the other, he is a cruel and vicious thief.

memoryHook.text: "ambi-" (both) + "val" (strength/feeling) = having strong feelings in both directions.

entries.etymology: French améliorer, from Old French meilleur ('better'), from Latin meliorare, from melior ('better')
entries.exampleSentence: The tense situation was ameliorated when Sam proposed a solution everyone could agree upon.

memoryHook.text: A million rate - taking a bad rating and improving it to a million.

entries.etymology: Anglo-French amenable, from Old French amener ('to lead to'), from a- + mener ('to lead'), from Latin minare ('to drive')
entries.exampleSentence: Our father was amenable when we asked him to drive us to the farm so we could go apple picking.

memoryHook.text: Amen-able - someone who frequently says "amen" (yes) and is easily persuaded.

entries.etymology: Latin amenitas ('pleasantness'), from amoenus ('pleasant')
entries.exampleSentence: Bill Gates's house is stocked with so many amenities, he never has to do anything for himself.

entries.etymology: Old French amiable, from Latin amicabilis ('friendly'), from amicus ('friend')
entries.exampleSentence: An amiable fellow, Harry got along with just about everyone.

memoryHook.text: "am-" (love/friend) = friendly and pleasant (used for people).

entries.etymology: Late Latin amicabilis, from Latin amicus ('friend'), from amare ('to love')
entries.exampleSentence: Claudia and Jimmy got divorced, but amicably and without hard feelings.

memoryHook.text: "am-" (love/friend) = friendly and peaceful (used for agreements/situations).

entries.etymology: Latin amorosus, from amor ('love'), from amare ('to love')
entries.exampleSentence: Whenever Albert saw Mariah wear her slinky red dress, he began to feel quite amorous.

memoryHook.text: "am-" (love) + "-ous" (full of) = full of romantic love.

entries.etymology: Greek amorphos, from a- ('without') + morphe ('form, shape')
entries.exampleSentence: The effort was doomed from the start, because the reasons behind it were so amorphous and hard to pin down.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (without) + "morph" (shape) = without definite shape.

entries.definition: Being out of correct chronological order
entries.etymology: Greek anachronismos, from ana- ('back') + chronos ('time')
entries.exampleSentence: In this book you're writing, you say that the Pyramids were built after the Titanic sank, which is anachronistic.

memoryHook.text: "ana-" (backward/against) + "chron" (time) = placed in the wrong time period.

entries.etymology: Greek analgesia ('absence of pain'), from an- ('without') + algos ('pain')
entries.exampleSentence: Put this analgesic on the wound so that the poor man at least feels a little better.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "alg" (pain) = medicine that takes away pain.

entries.definition: Similar to, so that an analogy can be drawn
entries.etymology: Greek analogos ('proportionate'), from ana- ('according to') + logos ('ratio, proportion')
entries.exampleSentence: Though they are unrelated genetically, the bone structure of whales and fish is quite analogous.

entries.definition: One who wants to eliminate all government
entries.etymology: Greek anarchos ('without a ruler'), from an- ('without') + archos ('ruler')
entries.exampleSentence: An anarchist, Carmine wanted to dissolve every government everywhere.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "arch" (ruler) = one who believes in no government.

entries.etymology: Greek anathema ('thing devoted to evil'), from anatithenai ('to set up'), from ana- + tithenai ('to place')
entries.exampleSentence: I never want to see that murderer. He is an anathema to me.

memoryHook.text: A nasty asthma - something you vehemently despise and avoid.

entries.etymology: Greek anekdotos ('unpublished'), from an- ('not') + ekdotos ('published'), from ekdidonai ('to publish')
entries.exampleSentence: After dinner, Marlon told an anecdote about the time he got his nose stuck in a toaster.

entries.etymology: Greek anaisthesia ('lack of sensation'), from an- ('without') + aisthesis ('sensation')
entries.exampleSentence: When the nerves in his spine were damaged, Mr. Hollins suffered anesthesia in his legs.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "esthes" (feeling) = without physical sensation.

entries.etymology: Old French angoisse, from Latin angustia ('tightness, distress'), from angustus ('narrow')
entries.exampleSentence: Angelos suffered terrible anguish when he learned that Buffy had died.

entries.etymology: Latin animatus, past participle of animare ('to give life to'), from anima ('breath, soul')
entries.exampleSentence: When he begins to talk about drama, which is his true passion, he becomes very animated.

memoryHook.text: "anima" (soul/life) = full of life.

entries.etymology: Latin annexare, from ad- + nectere ('to bind')

senses.exampleSentence: After defeating them in battle, the Russians annexed Poland.

senses.definition: A room attached to a larger room or space
senses.exampleSentence: He likes to do his studying in a little annex attached to the main reading room in the library.

entries.exampleSentence: After defeating them in battle, the Russians annexed Poland.

entries.etymology: Old French annuller, from Latin annullare, from ad- + nullus ('none')
entries.exampleSentence: After seeing its unforeseen and catastrophic effects, Congress sought to annul the law.

memoryHook.text: A null - making a contract legally null and void.

entries.definition: Something that does not fit into the normal order
entries.etymology: Greek anomalia ('unevenness'), from anomalos ('uneven'), from an- ('not') + homalos ('even')
entries.exampleSentence: "That rip in the spacetime continuum is certainly a spatial anomaly," said Spock to Captain Kirk.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (not) + "homos" (same) = not the same as the normal rule.

entries.etymology: Greek anonymos, from an- ('without') + onyma ('name')
entries.exampleSentence: Mary received a love poem from an anonymous admirer.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "onym" (name) = without a name.

entries.etymology: Greek antagonisma, from antagonizesthai ('to contend against'), from anti- + agonizesthai ('to contend')
entries.exampleSentence: Superman and Bizarro Superman shared a mutual antagonism, and often fought.

entries.etymology: Latin antecedere ('to go before'), from ante- ('before') + cedere ('to go')
entries.exampleSentence: The great tradition of Western culture had its antecedent in the culture of Ancient Greece.

memoryHook.text: "ante-" (before) + "ced" (go) = the thing that goes before.

entries.etymology: Latin ante ('before') + diluvium ('flood'), referring to the Biblical flood — before Noah's flood
entries.exampleSentence: The antediluvian man still believed that Eisenhower was president of the United States and that hot dogs cost a nickel.

memoryHook.text: "ante-" (before) + "diluvium" (flood) = literally "before the biblical flood," meaning ancient.

entries.definition: A selected collection of writings, songs, etc.
entries.etymology: Greek anthologia ('flower gathering'), from anthos ('flower') + legein ('to gather')
entries.exampleSentence: The new anthology of Bob Dylan songs contains all his greatest hits and a few songs you might never have heard before.

memoryHook.text: Greek anthos (flower) + logia (collection): a bouquet metaphor for chosen writings.

entries.etymology: Greek antipatheia, from anti- ('against') + pathos ('feeling')
entries.exampleSentence: I know you love me, but because you are a liar and a thief, I feel nothing but antipathy for you.

memoryHook.text: "anti-" (against) + "path" (feeling) = a strong feeling against someone.

entries.etymology: Latin antiquatus, past participle of antiquare ('to make old'), from antiquus ('ancient')
entries.exampleSentence: That antiquated car has none of the features, like power windows and steering, that make modern cars so great.

entries.etymology: Greek anti- ('against') + septikos ('putrefying'), from sepein ('to make rotten')
entries.exampleSentence: The antiseptic hospital was very bare, but its cleanliness helped to keep patients healthy.

memoryHook.text: "anti-" (against) + "septic" (infection) = against infection.

entries.etymology: Greek antithesis ('opposition'), from anti- ('against') + tithenai ('to place')
entries.exampleSentence: Your values, which hold war and violence in the highest esteem, are the antithesis of my pacifist beliefs.

memoryHook.text: "anti-" (opposite) + "thesis" (idea) = the exact opposite.

entries.etymology: Latin anxietas, from anxius ('troubled'), from angere ('to choke, distress')
entries.exampleSentence: When he heard about the car crash, he felt anxiety because he knew that his girlfriend had been driving on that road.

entries.etymology: Greek apathes ('without feeling'), from a- ('without') + pathos ('feeling')
entries.exampleSentence: Uninterested in politics, Bruno was apathetic about whether he lived under a capitalist or communist regime.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (without) + "path" (feeling) = without emotion or interest.

entries.etymology: Greek apokryphos ('hidden away'), from apo- ('away') + kryptein ('to hide')
entries.exampleSentence: Because I am standing before you, it seems obvious that the stories circulating about my demise were apocryphal.

memoryHook.text: A pocket full of lies - a story of doubtful authenticity.

entries.etymology: Old French apallir ('to grow pale'), from a- + pallir ('to be pale'), from Latin pallere

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumlocutio(n-), from circum 'around' + locutio.
entries.exampleSentence: The professor’s habit of speaking in circumlocutions made it difficult to follow his lectures.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "loc" (speak) = talking around the subject to avoid the point.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumscribere, from circum 'around' + scribere 'write'.
entries.exampleSentence: The children were permitted to play tag only within a carefully circumscribed area of the lawn.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "scribere" (write/draw) = drawing a line around something to restrict it.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumspectus, from circumspicere 'look around'.
entries.exampleSentence: Though I promised Rachel’s father I would bring her home promptly by midnight, it would have been more circumspect not to have specified a time.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "spect" (look) = looking around carefully before acting; cautious.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin circumvent- 'baffled, enclosed', from circumvenire.
entries.exampleSentence: The school’s dress code forbidding navel-baring jeans was circumvented by the determined students, who were careful to cover up with long coats when administrators were nearby.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "vent" (go) = to find a way around an obstacle.

entries.definition: Able to perceive things that normal people cannot
entries.etymology: Late 17th century: from French, from clair 'clear' + voyant 'seeing'.
entries.exampleSentence: Zelda’s uncanny ability to detect my lies was nothing short of clairvoyant.

memoryHook.text: "clair" (clear) + "voyant" (seeing) = having clear vision of the future.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French clamour, from Latin clamor.

senses.exampleSentence: Each morning the birds outside my window make such a clamor that they wake me up.
Neville’s fans clamored for him to appear on stage, but he had passed out on the floor of his dressing room.

memoryHook.text: Claim more - the angry mob is loudly shouting to claim more rights.

entries.exampleSentence: Each morning the birds outside my window make such a clamor that they wake me up.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from French clandestin or Latin clandestinus.
entries.exampleSentence: Announcing to her boyfriend that she was going to the gym, Sophie actually went to meet Joseph for a clandestine liaison.

memoryHook.text: Clan destiny - the secret clan held a hidden meeting to discuss their destiny.

entries.etymology: Old English clēofan 'split'.

senses.exampleSentence: Following the scandalous disgrace of their leader, the entire political party cleaved into warring factions.
After resolving their marital problems, Junior and Rosa cleaved to one another all the more tightly.

memoryHook.text: Meat cleaver - a heavy knife used to split things apart.

entries.exampleSentence: Following the scandalous disgrace of their leader, the entire political party cleaved into warring factions.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin clementia, from clemens, clement- 'merciful'.
entries.exampleSentence: After he forgot their anniversary, Martin could only beg Maria for clemency.

memoryHook.text: Lemon tea - giving the sore-throated prisoner a warm lemon tea as an act of mercy.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French clergie, based on ecclesiastical Latin clericus 'clergyman'.
entries.exampleSentence: Though the villagers viewed the church rectory as quaint and charming, the clergy who lived there regarded it as a mildewy and dusty place that aggravated their allergies.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from obsolete cloy 'to clog'.
entries.exampleSentence: Though Ronald was physically attractive, Maud found his constant compliments and solicitous remarks cloying.

memoryHook.text: Clawing - romantic words that are so overly sweet you want to claw your ears off.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin coagulat- 'curdled', from the verb coagulare.
entries.exampleSentence: The top layer of the pudding had coagulated into a thick skin.

memoryHook.text: Colgate - toothpaste thickens and solidifies if left out.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin coalescere, from co- 'together' + alescere 'grow up'.
entries.exampleSentence: Gordon’s ensemble of thrift-shop garments coalesced into a surprisingly handsome outfit.

memoryHook.text: Fuse into one: co- (together) + alescere (to grow)-parts grow together into a single mass.

entries.definition: A person who makes or repairs shoes
entries.etymology: Middle English: of unknown origin.
entries.exampleSentence: I had my neighborhood cobbler replace my worn-out leather soles with new ones.

entries.definition: To make somebody do something by force or threat
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin coercere 'restrain'.
entries.exampleSentence: The court decided that Vanilla Ice did not have to honor the contract because he had been coerced into signing it.

memoryHook.text: Force - using force or threats to get someone to do something.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin cogent- 'compelling', from the verb cogere.
entries.exampleSentence: Irene’s arguments in favor of abstinence were so cogent that I could not resist them.

memoryHook.text: Co-agent - your FBI co-agent presents a clear, logical, and convincing argument.

entries.etymology: Early 19th century: from obsolete cognizance 'knowledge' + -ant.
entries.exampleSentence: Jake avoided speaking to women in bars because he was cognizant of the fact that drinking impairs his judgment.

memoryHook.text: "cogn" (know) = having knowledge or awareness.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin cohaerent- 'sticking together'.
entries.exampleSentence: Renee could not figure out what Monroe had seen because he was too distraught to deliver a coherent statement.

memoryHook.text: "co-" (together) + "haerere" (stick) = sticking together logically.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin collateralis, from col- 'together with' + lateralis.

senses.exampleSentence: Divorcing my wife had the collateral effect of making me poor, as she was the only one of us with a job or money.
Jacob left his watch as collateral for the $500 loan.

entries.exampleSentence: Divorcing my wife had the collateral effect of making me poor, as she was the only one of us with a job or money.

entries.etymology: Mid 18th century: from Latin colloquium 'conversation' + -al.
entries.exampleSentence: Adam’s essay on sexual response in primates was marked down because it contained too many colloquial expressions.

memoryHook.text: "col-" (together) + "loqu" (speak) = how people speak together in everyday conversation.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin collusio(n-), from the verb colludere.
entries.exampleSentence: The three law students worked in collusion to steal the final exam.

memoryHook.text: "col-" (together) + "ludere" (play/deceive) = playing together in a secret, deceitful plot.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin, from Greek kolossos.
entries.exampleSentence: For 56 years, the ancient city of Rhodes featured a colossus standing astride its harbor.

entries.definition: The act or process of burning
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin combustio(n-), from comburere 'burn up'.
entries.exampleSentence: The unexpected combustion of the prosecution’s evidence forced the judge to dismiss the case against Ramirez.

entries.definition: A notice of approval or recognition
entries.etymology: Middle English: from Latin commendatio(n-), from the verb commendare.
entries.exampleSentence: Jared received a commendation from Linda, his supervisor, for his stellar performance.

memoryHook.text: Recommend a nation - giving official praise.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from late Latin commensuratus, from com- 'together' + mensuratus.
entries.exampleSentence: Ahab selected a very long roll and proceeded to prepare a tuna salad sandwich commensurate with his enormous appetite.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (together) + "mensur" (measure) = corresponding in size or degree.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin commodiosus, from Latin commodus 'convenient'.
entries.exampleSentence: Holden invited the three women to join him in the back seat of the taxicab, assuring them that the car was quite commodious.

memoryHook.text: Commode (toilet) - a massive, spacious bathroom.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin compellere, from com- 'together' + pellere 'drive'.
entries.exampleSentence: Eliot’s speech was so compelling that Lenore accepted his proposal on the spot.

entries.definition: To make an appropriate payment for something
entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin compensat- 'weighed against', from the verb compensare.
entries.exampleSentence: Reginald bought Sharona a new dress to compensate her for the one he’d spilled his ice cream on.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin complacentia, from Latin complacere 'to please'.
entries.exampleSentence: Colin tried to shock his friends out of their complacency by painting a frightening picture of what might happen to them.

memoryHook.text: Come play, senor - relaxing and playing instead of staying alert to danger.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin complementum, from complere 'fill up'.
entries.exampleSentence: Ann’s scarf complements her blouse beautifully, making her seem fully dressed even though she isn’t wearing a coat.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (together) + "plere" (fill) = to fill up together, completing the whole.

entries.definition: Ready to adapt oneself to another’s wishes
entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from comply + -ant.
entries.exampleSentence: Sue had very strong opinions about what to do on a first date, and Ted was absolutely compliant.

entries.definition: Being an accomplice in a wrongful act
entries.etymology: Mid 20th century: back-formation from complicity.
entries.exampleSentence: By keeping her daughter’s affair a secret, Maddie became complicit in it.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (together) + "plic" (fold) = folded together into an illegal act.

entries.definition: An expression of esteem or approval
entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from French compliment, from Italian complimento.
entries.exampleSentence: I blushed crimson when Emma gave me a compliment on my new haircut.

memoryHook.text: Latin complementum (what completes); courtesy praise - remember: I like compliments.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French compoune, from Latin componere 'put together'.

senses.exampleSentence: The difficulty of finding a fire escape amid the smoke was compounded with the dangers posed by the panicking crowds.
My attraction to Donna was a compound of curiosity about the unknown, physical desire, and intellectual admiration.

senses.definition: A walled area containing a group of buildings
senses.exampleSentence: When the fighting started, Joseph rushed into the family compound because it was safe and well defended.

entries.exampleSentence: The difficulty of finding a fire escape amid the smoke was compounded with the dangers posed by the panicking crowds.

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from French compréhensif, -ive, or late Latin comprehensivus.
entries.exampleSentence: She sent me a comprehensive list of the ingredients needed to cook rabbit soufflé.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French compresser or Latin compressare, frequentative of comprimere.
entries.exampleSentence: Lynn compressed her lips into a frown.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French componction, from ecclesiastical Latin compunctio(n-).
entries.exampleSentence: He felt compunction for the shabby way he’d treated her.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (intensive) + "punct" (prick) = a sharp prick of guilt.

entries.etymology: Late 15th century: from French concéder or Latin concedere, from con- 'completely' + cedere 'yield'.
entries.exampleSentence: Andrew had to concede that what his mother said about Diana made sense.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (completely) + "cedere" (yield) = to completely yield the argument.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from late Latin conciliatorius, from conciliare 'combine, gain'.
entries.exampleSentence: I took Amanda’s invitation to dinner as a very conciliatory gesture.

memoryHook.text: Council - bringing the arguing sides to the council to make peace.

entries.etymology: Late 16th century: from French concis or Latin concisus, past participle of concidere 'cut up, cut down'.
entries.exampleSentence: Gordon did not like to waste time, and his instructions to Brenda were nothing if not concise.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (completely) + "caedere" (cut) = cut down to just the essential words.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin concoct- 'cooked together, digested', from concoquere.
entries.exampleSentence: She concocted the most ridiculous story to explain her absence.

memoryHook.text: Cocktail - mixing ingredients together to invent a new cocktail.

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin concomitant- 'accompanying', from concomitari.
entries.exampleSentence: His dislike of hard work carried with it a concomitant lack of funds.

memoryHook.text: Comet tent - if a comet hits your tent, disaster is a naturally accompanying result.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French concorde, from Latin concordia, from concors 'of one mind'.
entries.exampleSentence: Julie and Harold began the evening with a disagreement, but ended it in a state of perfect concord.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "cord" (heart) = hearts together in harmony.

entries.definition: An expression of sympathy in sorrow
entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin condolere 'suffer together' + -ence.
entries.exampleSentence: Brian lamely offered his condolences on the loss of his sister’s roommate’s cat.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "dolere" (grieve) = to grieve with someone.

entries.etymology: Mid 19th century: from Latin condonare 'refrain from exacting'.
entries.exampleSentence: He refused to condone his brother’s crime.

memoryHook.text: Con done - the convict is done with his sentence, so you overlook his past offense.

entries.definition: A pipe or channel through which something passes
entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French, from medieval Latin conductus 'pipe, channel'.
entries.exampleSentence: The water flowed through the conduit into the container.

memoryHook.text: Can do it - the pipe is the channel that can do the job of moving the water.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Latin confectio(n-), from conficere 'put together'.
entries.exampleSentence: We went to the mall food court and purchased a delicious confection.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: alteration of confident (as a noun), probably to represent the pronunciation of French confidente.
entries.exampleSentence: Shortly after we met, she became my chief confidant.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "fid" (trust) = the person you completely trust with your secrets.

entries.etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin conflagratio(n-), from the verb conflagrare 'burn up'.
entries.exampleSentence: The conflagration consumed the entire building.

memoryHook.text: Flag ration - a massive fire burned through the military base, so they had to ration their remaining flags.
